---
title: "Including non-academics in evaluation"
author: "Experiments in Assessment WG"
date: 2026-04-09
date-modified: last-modified
categories: ["Level 0", "Aim: Value process", "Aim: Inclusivity", "Aim: Alternative questions", "CoARA Commitment 1", "CoARA Commitment 2", "Target: Funder", "Target: Meta-researcher"]
description: "Including non-academics in evaluation broadens perspectives by integrating stakeholders from industry, policy, and society. It enhances relevance, transparency, and societal impact, mitigates disciplinary bias, and supports responsible research assessment aligned with real-world needs and principles of openness, inclusiveness, and accountability."
image: false
---

::: {.callout-warning icon=false}

## Objectives and potential outcome

:::

## Research domains

This is especially relevant for funding evaluation, especially when assessing impacts, but it could also be relevant in a variety of domains and settings. 

## Context and considerations

This idea is connected with the following ideas where further details can be found:
- [Community impact evaluation](https://eui-library.github.io/coara-idea-catalogue/ideas/community-impact-evaluation/index.html)
- [Multidimensional “diversity” in evaluation committees](https://eui-library.github.io/coara-idea-catalogue/ideas/multidimensional-diversity-in-evaluation-committees/)
- [Non-expert panels](https://eui-library.github.io/coara-idea-catalogue/ideas/nonexpert-panels/)
- [Research users impacted as evaluators](https://eui-library.github.io/coara-idea-catalogue/ideas/research-users-impacted-as-evaluators/)


## Challenges and mitigations

## Evaluating success

## Related CoARA commitments

# Relevant resources and literature 

This section includes resources, literature, and reports relevant to this specific experimental idea.

## Templates from funders and institutions 

## Case examples and literature

Several funders involve members of the public in their review panels, see for example [NIHR](https://www.nihr.ac.uk/get-involved/read-and-review-documentation/public-reviewers) and [Health Research Board Ireland](https://www.hrb.ie/funding/responsible-research-assessment/public-and-patient-involvement-in-research/ppi-information-for-the-public/).

Luo, J., Ma, L., Shankar, K. (2021). **[Does the inclusion of non-academic reviewers make any difference for grant impact panels?](https://doi.org/10.1093/scipol/scab046). *Science and Public Policy*, 48(6), pp. 763–775.

Junwen Luo (2021, 25 August), **[What role should non-academics have in evaluating the potential impact of new research projects?](https://blogs.lse.ac.uk/impactofsocialsciences/2021/08/25/what-role-should-non-academics-have-in-evaluating-the-potential-impact-of-new-research-projects/)** LSE Impact Blog .

Baxter S, Clowes M, Muir D, Baird W, Broadway-Parkinson A, Bennett C. (2016). **[Supporting public involvement in interview and other panels: a systematic review](https://doi.org/10.1111/hex.12491)**. *Health Expect*, 20(5). p. 807-817.

A blog post that describes the experience as a public member of a research funding panel: Adam Forristal Golberg (2025, 25 February), **[A peek inside a research funding panel](https://socialscienceresearchfunding.co.uk/?p=1455)**. *Cash for Questions: Research funding, policy and development*.


## Other resources


# Case Studies or Implementation Examples
